observations.recommendation: We reiterated the prior year’s recommendation that Management instruct the – a. Municipal Treasurer to • update in the RPTAR the running balance of delinquent taxpayers based on the assessment roll provided by the Municipal Assessor’s Office; and • implement procedures and strategies to further improve collection efficiency through publishing yearly the notice of delinquency once a week for two consecutive weeks in a newspaper of general circulation; and including remarks/statements regarding the reason/s for non-collection or non-payment in the certified list of all real property tax delinquencies which remained uncollected or unpaid for at least one year, with a request for assistance in the enforcement of the remedies for collection to be submitted to the sanggunian; b. Municipal Accountant – • consider requesting to the Commission on Audit’s Accounting Systems Development and Other Services Office (ASDOSO) under the Government Accountancy Sector to install the latest version of enhanced New Government Accounting System (e-NGAS) which will aid them in recording transactions, financial statement preparation, among others, but also to facilitate the maintenance of Subsidiary Ledgers related to RPT/ SET Receivables to provide reconciliation with records of MTO as well as to support the balance reflected in the financial statements. c. Municipal Treasurer – • request for the replacement of the resigned employee assigned in the Municipal Treasury Office (MTO) to fast track the encoding and updating of the details included in the RPTAR; and • continue to extend further actions to enforce the payment of delinquent taxpayers and increase the effort to increase percentage of collection of receivables; and enforce payment from delinquent taxpayers.
observations.managementAction: Not implemented. The Audit Team conducted a validation by sampling hard copies of RPTARs (index cards). The validation revealed an amount of undisclosed delinquency, totaling ₱2,580,355.65. This amount was found within two sampled drawers, where the RPTARs were kept, which contained delinquent taxpayers. The sampled drawers represented only a portion of the total RPTAR records. To speed up and streamline the process of encoding and updating RPTAR data, two (2) JO employees were hired and assigned to the Municipal Treasurer’s Office (MTO) on April 1, 2024, replacing the resigned employee who previously handled these tasks. The Certified List of All Real Property Tax Delinquencies was submitted to and received by the Commission on Audit (COA) of Orion on April 29, 2024. This submission followed a great deal of hard work and effort by both the Offices of the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to reconcile discrepancies amounting to ₱40,333,286.97. These discrepancies concerned the recorded amount of delinquent real taxpayers as of year-end, which was noted during the audit. As required, the notice of delinquency was published once a week for two consecutive weeks in a newspaper of general circulation in Bataan. Copies were provided by the Provincial Treasurer’s Office (PTO) to the Municipal Treasurer’s Office (MTO). The MTO of Orion also issued notices to delinquent taxpayers and worked with barangays to post the list locally to improve collection. The suggestion to indicate reasons for non-payment or non-collection has been acknowledged and considered. The Office of the Municipal Accountant contacted the Commission on Audit’s Accounting Systems Development and Other Services Office (ASDOSO) regarding the enhanced eNGAS. The COA ASDOSO replied in an email dated April 28, 2025 placing the municipality in the waiting list while the latter is preparing for the data and technical requirements prior to the training of the accounting and budget staff and IT personnel, respectively. The installation of the new version will address changes to the chart of accounts and other accounting policies as well as improve efficiency in recording and maintaining records. The training request was forwarded to the Provincial Government of Bataan (PGB) and is currently awaiting confirmation of its scheduling in 2025. The municipality is currently on the official waiting list for the next scheduled training on Enhanced eNGAS version 2.0. JEV No. 2024-12-004534 and JEV-2024-12-001042 were prepared and recorded last year through JEV-2024-12-004534 and JEV-2024-12-001042 respectively. Additionally, as recommended, further adjusting entries were prepared on July 31, 2025 (JEV-2025-07-002354 for RPT Receivable and JEV-2025-07-000602 for SET Receivable) to record the unreported RPT delinquency and to correct a systematic error in the formula applied to a real property located in Sto. Domingo. However, as of this writing, the issue remains unresolved.

observations.managementAction: Implemented. The understatement of inventory accounts attributed to the RHU Pharmacist totaling ₱1,870,572.17, was already rectified. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the MOA which was the basis of the treatment of collections from PCSO for verification of the Audit Team, otherwise, record the municipal share on STL as Share from PCSO subject to budgetary restrictions and requirements in the General Fund and return the unused balances of the proceeds from Trust Fund to General Fund.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Mayor instruct the Project Coordinators to submit to the Auditor’s Office copies of perfected MOA and Project Proposals for all fund transfers for the latter to verify if such funds were used in accordance with the intended purpose.

We reiterated that the Municipal Accountant (a) ascertain completeness of the supporting documents, information and signatures for every transaction before the same is processed, approved and paid; (b) stop the payment of telephone expenses by means of communication allowance paid to school principals; and (c) make all payments by checks directly to creditors, except payments in small amounts which may be made by cash through the petty cash fund as specified in Items 2.2 and 2.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002.

We reiterated that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Development Council to optimally use the 20% DF to implement the identified social, economic and environmental management PPAs as planned.

We recommended that the (a) Acting Municipal Mayor fill up the mandatory positions in the MDRRM Office; (b) designated MDRRMO (i) implement and monitor the planned PPAs in the LDRRMIP; and (ii) prepare and submit a certified monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on or before the 15th day of the following month; and (c) Municipal Accountant (i) record the rescue vehicle and equipment purchased in the General Fund; and (ii) transfer the unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund at the end of the year.

We commended the Management for the actions taken to implement RA No. 9003.  We recommended that the Solid Waste Management Implementing Group (a) speed up the operation of the central MRF and fully close the controlled dumpsite; (b) strictly enforce the penalties for the violators of RA No. 9003 and other environmental ordinances; and (c) prioritize and implement the approved Ten-Year SWMP in accordance with the requirements of RA No. 9003.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to (a) withhold and remit the total amount due to the GSIS within the 10th day of the following month; and (b) follow provisions of the GAA on the employee’s net take home pay.

We recommended that the Acting Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to (a) withhold and remit the total amount due to the Pag-IBIG within the prescribed period; and (b) follow provisions of Section 47 of the GAA pertaining to mandatory deductions from the employee’s salary and the required net take home pay.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant always remind the persons liable for the disallowance to settle their liability in accordance with the installment schedule approved by the Commission Proper.

We reiterated the prior year’s recommendation that Municipal Mayor (a) require the Municipal Treasurer to deposit all collections intact daily or not later than the next banking day; and (b) establish a proper monitoring and control procedures on collections and deposits to avoid the risk of loss/misplacement and misuse/misappropriation of government money.
observations.reason: Collections remained not deposited intact due to unavailability of service vehicle and some were granted to officials and employees as vales/IOUs. Reiterated in Observation No. 3

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our previous year’s recommendations that Management (a) create an inventory committee to conduct actual physical inventory and further verification to reconcile the balance of the PPE account as presented in the financial statements and inventory report; (b) create an appraisal committee to revalue properties with no costs; (c) require the Municipal Accountant to maintain PPELC to monitor the whereabouts and status of PPE; and (d) accomplish the required IIRUPs for the unserviceable and condemnable equipment indicating the manner of disposition.
observations.reason:
The Local Chief Executive did not addressed the creation of inventory and appraisal committee. Maintenance of PPELCs and PCs was not adopted by the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer.

Reiterated in Observation No. 1

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management strictly comply with the provisions of Section 7.2.1(a) of the RRSA, Section 39 of PD No. 1445, Section 23 (1) (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987 and other laws and regulations on submission and rendering of accounts.
observations.reason:
DVs, collection and liquidation reports were submitted late due to delayed submission of supporting documents by the different departments.

Reiterated in Observation No. 11

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management (a) establish clear-cut policy on monitoring and implementation of BuB projects, particularly microfinance from DSWD; and (b) install internal control in recording and deposit of collections in order to avoid wastage of government meager resources and attain project objectives.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality will schedule talks with DSWD with regards to the recording and deposits of collections from beneficiaries.
observations.reason:
The Municipality did not make any representations with source agencies regarding the monitoring and implementation of BuB projects.

Reiterated in Observation No. 8

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management (a) review and update annually the LDRRMFIP to include activities to be funded from the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of the previous year’s transferred to the Special Trust Fund account; (b) adhere to the accounting and reporting guidelines of the LDRRMF to properly account for the expenses related thereto; and (c) direct the OIC-MDRRMO to (i) prepare a certified monthly Report on the Utilization of DRRMF and submit to COA on or before the 15th day after the end of each month; and (ii) monitor and evaluate the utilization of the LDRRMF based on the programs, projects and activities listed in the LDRRMIP as incorporated in the annual investment plan to ensure the full implementation of the pre-disaster preparedness programs.
observations.reason:
LDRRMF was still utilized on expenditures not related to calamity. LDRRM reports was not prepared and submitted within the given timelines. The unutilized balance was not yet transferred to the Special Trust Fund account.

Reiterated in Observation No. 9

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the municipal share on sales proceeds of the PCSO Small Town Lottery (STL) as income in the General Fund to be made available for appropriation.

We also recommended that management revert the unexpended balances of collections amounting to ₱596,415.85 from the Trust Fund to the General Fund.
observations.reason: The LCE stated that there is a MOA between the Municipality and PCSO on the treatment of STL collections.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated that the municipality procure its common-use supplies from PS-DBM and secure a certificate of non-availability if common-use supplies were source from alternative suppliers.

We also recommended that management submit the APP-CSE on or before November 15 of each year to DBM Regional Office No. III.
observations.reason: Management commented that goods procured at PS-DBM were often sub-standard and usually not-on-hand.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management create an appraisal committee to determine value of donated road lots and require recording in the agency’s books thereafter.
observations.managementAction: Necessary documents are not on hand to support recording of the assets in the books of accounts.
observations.reason: The previous administration did not turnover any documents related to donated road lots to the present administration.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the management should take up in the books the public market and loans payable to fairly present the municipality’s financial condition.
observations.managementAction: The agency cannot act on this observation because no ESF loan was availed to fund the public market.
observations.reason: The management claimed that they did not avail an ESF loan to fund the public market. According to them, if they have an ESF loan, the financial institution who lent them the fund will force them to pay the said loan.

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our recommendation and the Management agreed that the (a) LCE (i) create an inventory committee to conduct physical count of all properties; and (ii) create an appraisal committee to determine the value of the properties with no costs; and (b) Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer complete and update the PPELC and PC by class and/or type and reconcile the balance of the PPE accounts per books and property records.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Mayor assign personnel who will help the Municipal Treasurer in updating the list of taxpayers; (b) Municipal Accountant request for write-off of RPT/SET Receivables after exerting all efforts in reconciling the said accounts; (c) Municipal Treasurer (i) follow up the sent files for list of delinquent accounts with the barangays by coordinating with the Barangay Captain who has the jurisdiction thereof; and (ii) submit to the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list of taxpayers to evade the incorrect set-up of RPT/SET Receivables at the beginning of each year.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the (a) Municipal Mayor (i) instruct the Municipal Treasurer to immediately deposit collections and avoid keeping large amount of money in the vault; and (ii) secure authority from the SB to close the PNB account to prevent dormancy and incurrence of bank service charges; and (b) Municipal Treasurer (i) stop using collections for the payment of reimbursements, cash advances, vales/IOUs, among others, and deposit immediately all collections received daily or not later than the next banking day; and (ii) designate a personnel to prepare and submit monthly RAAFs.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the (a) Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer (a) ensure that the cash advances and liquidations of payroll of the Municipality strictly comply with the provisions of Section 194 of the GAAM, Volume I and Item 1.2.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001; (b) HRMO include all casual and job order employees in the biometric system; and (c) MITO transfer to the HRMO the operation of the biometric system as ordered by the LCE.

We recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant (i) cause the payment through checks of all disbursements, except payments in small amounts, as specified in Item 2.2 of COA Circular No. 97-002; and (ii) ascertain completeness of the supporting documents and signatures in every transactions before the same are processed.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Treasurer make all payments by checks directly to suppliers and ensure that only small amounts are paid by cash through the petty cash fund as provided for in Items 2.2 and 2.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002; and (b) Members of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) comply strictly with the rules and regulations of RA No. 9184 to avoid evasion of the bidding process and resorting to alternative procurement procedures.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor effect the full tranches of salary/wage increase of municipal officials and employees before giving additional allowances to national government officials in compliance with rules and regulations.

We recommended and the Management agreed that (a) Project Coordinators (i) ensure that all BuB projects are implemented in accordance with the duly approved MOA and project proposal and the proposed budget are specified in detail; and (ii) make representation with the DILG Region III for the perfected MOA of the Evacuation Facility, Salintubig and LGSF projects; (b) MSWDO submit the revised guidelines and/or project proposal of the SLP SEA-K Scheme; and (c) Municipal Accountant, Municipal Engineer and BAC Chairman ensure that all documentary requirements are attached in the disbursement vouchers, supported by sufficient information and signed by authorized officials and employees before payment as evidence of the validity of transactions.

We reiterated that the (a) OIC-MDRRMO (i) monitor and evaluate the utilization of LDRRMF based on the programmed PPAs in the LDRRMIP as incorporated in the annual investment plan to ensure the full implementation of the pre-disaster preparedness PPAs to avoid the underutilization thereof; (ii) prepare a certified monthly Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF and submit the report  on or before the 15th day after the end of each month; and (iii) review and update annually the LDRRMFIP to include activities to be funded from the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of the previous years; (b) Municipal Accountant (i) adhere to the accounting and reporting guidelines of the LDRRMF to properly account the expenses related thereto; (ii) transfer the unexpended/unobligated balance of the QRF and the DRRMF-MOOE to the Special Trust Fund at the end of the year; and (iii) ascertain completeness of the supporting documents, information and signatures before payments are processed; and (c) Officer-In-Charge-Municipal Budget Officer avoid charging expenses not related to the LDRRMF.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the (a) Municipal Development Council to identify PPAs which are the top priority of the Municipality to avoid change of plans and re-programming of unimplemented projects; (b) Municipal Planning and Development Officer and Municipal Engineer to (i) always supervise the status of PPA implementation so that vital changes may be presented before time; and (ii) create feasible timetable to evade delays in implementing the development projects; and (c) Municipal Accountant to maintain special accounts under the General Fund for development projects.

We recommended that the (a) Municipal Mayor (i) assign additional competent personnel in the Accounting Department; and (ii) instruct the other departments of the agency to submit the necessary SDs to the DVs to avoid delayed submission of the latter; and (b) Municipal Accountant (i) submit to the Auditor’s Office the accounts, financial records and required reports within the prescribed period; and (ii) strictly comply with the provisions of Section 7.2.1 of the RRSA, Sections 23 (1) and (3) of the Administrative Code of 1987 and other laws, rules and regulations on submission and rendering of accounts to avoid suspension and eventually, disallowance of the Municipality’s transactions.

We commended Management for the significant efforts made to implement RA No. 9003.  We recommended that extra effort still be exerted and the (a) Solid Waste Management Implementing Group (i) speed up the operation of the Sanitary Landfill since the submitted SCRP was good as approved by the EMB; (ii) strictly enforce the penalties for the violators of “KASUNDUAN”, CSWMCO, RA No. 9003, Municipal Ordinance No. 12-020-077 and No Collection/No Segregation Policy; and (iii) make representation with the NSWMC for the approval of the 10-year plan; (b) OIC-MENRO prioritize the implementation of the requirements of RA No. 9003 and RA No. 8749 particularly the prohibition of open burning of solid wastes; and (c) Municipal Agriculturist encourage farmers to do composting of farm waste, instead of burning, to enrich the soil with natural fertilizers.

We reiterated our recommendation and the Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor, thru the MPDO Officer/GFPS TWG Chair and MSWD Officer/GFPS TWG Secretariat, (a) fully establish the Municipality’s GAD database to serve as basis for gender-responsive, planning, programming and policy formulation; and (b) strictly comply with the guidelines and procedures embodied in the JMC.

We recommended that the (a) OTFRB (i) strictly implement the Municipal Ordinance on the regulation of the operations of three-wheeled public conveyances along the Roman Super-highway within the locality to prevent undesirable consequences which might endanger both pedestrians and motorists; (ii) impose fines and penalties on tricycles operating their routes on national highways; and (iii) continuously conduct information campaign on the provision of operating conditions for tricycles; and (b) Municipal Engineer and Head of Orion Command Center to (i) inspect the condition of existing CCTVs and evaluate the need for repair and/or replacement thereof; (ii) assess the technical specifications of the CCTV needed in order to maximize the benefits of their utilization, taking into consideration the cost effectiveness and performance efficiency; (iii) install some of the proposed additional units within the national highway of the Municipality to provide  protection, monitoring and record purposes and for the promotion of public safety and order and immediate rescue and assistance to the public in case of accidents.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Accountant (a) pay reimbursements, net of withholding tax on procurement of goods and services; and (b) continue the effort of deducting the corresponding taxes from compensation of their officials and employees and all payments to suppliers/contractors and remitting the same within the prescribed period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, through the Municipal Accountant, require the persons determined liable for the disallowance to settle their liability since the prescriptive period within which to file a motion for reconsideration with the Commission Proper as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2009-006 dated September 15, 2009 has already elapsed.

We commended the Municipal Accountant for the thorough review and adjustments made to correct the balances. We still recommended that she continue all the efforts started in undertaking a thorough review of the encoding and journalizing of RPT/SET collections in view of the discrepancy between the RPT/SET Receivable and Deferred RPT/SET Income accounts and appropriately draw a journal entry voucher to take the necessary adjustments.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer submit to the Municipal Accountant a duly certified list showing the names of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year pursuant to Section 20 of the Manual on the NGAS to avoid the improper set-up of RPT/SET Receivable and Deferred RPT/SET Income accounts.
observations.managementAction: Review of the entries made was stopped since no journal entry voucher was left behind during the review in CY 2015. The Management will request for the write-off of the discrepancy.
observations.reason:
As of CY 2016, the unreconciled balance between the RPT/SET Receivables stood at ₱860,190.80. According to the Municipal Treasurer, the Assessment Roll of the Municipality for CY 2016 was not provided by the Provincial Capitol of Bataan thus, non-submission of duly certified list of taxpayers to the Municipal Accountant.

Reiterated in Observation No. 2

observations.recommendation: We reiterated prior years’ recommendation that Management limit the granting of honoraria to national government officials unless the municipality has already implemented the New Salary Standardization Rates for Philippine Government Employees and new RATA rates under DBM LBC No. 103 dated May 15, 2013.
observations.managementAction: The 2nd tranche of the Salary Standardization Law (SSL) was implemented on January 2016. The Municipality still grants allowances to other national government officials chargeable against Other Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses.
observations.reason:
Aside from financial constraints of the Municipality, the former Municipal Mayor did not implement immediately the salary/wage increase for CY 2010.

Reiterated in Observation No. 7

observations.recommendation: We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that Management (a) comply strictly with the mandates of COA Accounting Circular No. 2006-001 dated November 9, 2006 and Section 194 of the GAAM, Volume I; and (b) implement measures on the advantages of the secured Biometric System.
observations.managementAction:
Salary schedules have been adjusted to avoid off-setting of the salary differences of casual and job order employees. Instead of the normal 15-30 schedule, the salary of job order and casual employees are now given on the 20th of the current month and the 5th day of the following month.

A “No DTR – No Salary” rule has been implemented to further avoid the off-setting practice and instill responsibility and discipline to employees.

The Accounting Office has assigned a personnel to handle the DTRs of employees and take responsibilities on such upon transmittal from the HRMO.

With the non-transfer of the operation of the biometric to the HRMO, a closer coordination with the Municipal Information Technology Office (MITO) has been adapted to facilitate the order and timely downloading and monitoring of employee attendance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board headed by the Local Chief Executive (a) prioritize the operation of a Sanitary Landfill especially upon approval of the Safe Closure and Rehabilitation of Disposal Facilities and other requirements of RA No. 9003; (b) involve closely the barangay officials in the strict implementation of the existing municipal solid waste management ordinances and environmental laws so that efficient management of solid waste will be effectively enforced; and (c) implement the Ten-Year Solid Waste Management Plan.
observations.reason:
The implementation of Safe Closure Rehabilitation Plan (SCRP) was still on-going while the 10-Year Solid Waste Management Plan (SWMP) was not yet approved.

Reiterated in Observation No. 12

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous recommendation that the Local Chief Executive, in coordination with the GFPS, establish GAD Code, GAD Database and processes provided by the guidelines and strengthen the GFPS to accelerate gender mainstreaming within the locality and ensure that all issues and concerns will be addressed in pursuit of women-empowerment and gender equal locality.

We also recommended that Management strictly expend the GAD funds in accordance with the approved Annual GAD Plan and Budget.
observations.reason:
The Municipality’s GAD database was no yet fully established.

Reiterated in Observation No. 13

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) instruct the BAC members and the Municipal Engineer to properly plan and schedule all procurement activities pertaining to development projects to pave the way for their timely implementation and completion; and (b) ensure that the Municipal Planning and Development Officer and Municipal Engineer regularly monitor the status of project implementation so that necessary interventions may be introduced at the earliest time.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain special accounts under the General Fund for development projects for proper accountability and control.

We further recommended that BAC conduct mandatory review and evaluation of the terms, conditions and specifications in the bidding documents when there is a failure of bidding in consonance with Section 35.2 of RA No. 9184 Implementing Rules and Regulations.
observations.managementAction:
All government projects implemented by the Municipality were planned and monitored carefully but due to occurrence of unforeseeable things which sometimes hamper the completion of projects on time.

The unutilized portion of 20% Development Fund was due to the conscious effort of the Municipality to save fund for Solid Waste Management projects.
observations.reason:
20% DF was not optimally utilized as of year-end due to the saving of fund for Solid Waste Management projects.

Reiterated in Observation No. 10

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that management provide at least 10% of the SSIP project cost as counterpart from the LGU in the form of right-of-way cost and watershed development of at least one (1) hectare by planting agro-forest trees above the waterline of the reservoir as agreed in the MOA with BSWM and DA-RFU III.

Henceforth, we recommended that management conform to Revised IRR of RA 9184 on all their procurements.

We also recommended that management require the Municipal Accountant to record the rehabilitation/construction of SSIP, as follows:

Under Trust Fund books, to record the transfer of completed SSIP to Public Infrastructure:

Public Infrastructures
4,864,332.96

Credit
Irrigation, Canals and Laterals
4,864,332.96

4,864,332.96

Credit

4,864,332.96

To record the rehabilitation/construction of SSIP in the Registry of Public Infrastructures (RPI) under the GF books at the end of the year:

observations.managementAction: Liquidation report was already forwarded to BSWM.
observations.reason: The SSIP project was the brainchild of previous administration. As such, the current administration did not prioritize the implementation of the supposed counterpart of the LGU.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management submit liquidation report supported by necessary documents/data; procure goods through public bidding and pay directly through check; and require the Municipal Accountant to take up the expenses made from the fund transfers before taking up the liquidation in the books and to deduct the required taxes.
observations.managementAction: Funds for supplemental feeding were released to Day Care parents on a two-weeks requirement basis through a cash advance in the name of the Municipal Treasurer.
observations.reason: Liquidations were already supported with necessary documents. However, procurement of goods were still not coursed thru public bidding.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management of the Municipality of Orion verify the liquidation report submitted by the NGO. Substantiate the reasonableness of the cost of the Barangay Peace and Order Kit of ₱9,900.00 per unit. List of recipients should be attached to the report of disbursements.
observations.managementAction: The management always follows up the liquidation report on the address of the NGO as written in the official receipt. However, no reply has been received. Some of the Barangay Peace and Order Kits were distributed to nearby municipalities, the previous Municipal DILG as in-charge.
observations.reason: Only partial documents were submitted by the PPDFI to the municipality. Management will not be able to require the full liquidation report of the Barangay Peace and Order Kit Project since the address of the foundation has been verified by Special Audit Office (SAO) as non-existing. Management however, should substantiate the reasonableness of the cost of the Barangay Peace and Order Kit of ₱9,900.00 per unit and submit list of recipients.
